title: Harira
subtitle: Moroccan chickpea and lentil soup
excerpt: A thick, spiced Moroccan soup of chickpeas, red lentils, and tomatoes, fragrant with cinnamon and coriander and finished with lemon juice. The soup traditionally served to break the Ramadan fast — filling, warming, and very good. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Harira is the national soup of Morocco, eaten daily during Ramadan and for special occasions throughout the year. Documented by Paula Wolfert in Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco (1973) and by Claudia Roden in A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968). The soup varies by region and family; this version follows the Fes style with red lentils and a chickpea base.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. Reheat gently and add fresh lemon and herbs when serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps refrigerated for four days. Thickens considerably as it sits — loosen with water or stock when reheating.

recipeTools.notes: For a smoother, thicker soup. Leave unblended for a chunkier version.

content.text: Harira's thickness comes from the red lentils, which dissolve as they cook and turn the broth into something more substantial. The cinnamon and ginger are not background notes — they are part of the character of the soup. Do not reduce them. The lemon juice is added at the end and makes a considerable difference to the final flavour; the soup needs that acidity to lift the earthiness of the lentils and chickpeas.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion and {{celery}} chopped celery stalks for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger; stir for 1 minute. Add {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes; cook for 30 seconds. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes and stir well. Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils and {{chickpeas-tinned}} of drained chickpeas. Pour over 1 litre of water and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, and simmer uncovered for 25–30 minutes until the lentils have dissolved and the soup is thick. Stir in the lemon juice and taste for seasoning. Serve scattered with coriander.

items.cause: Not enough lentils, or too much water added.
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for an extra 10 minutes. The lentils should dissolve completely and thicken the broth — stir regularly to prevent sticking.

items.cause: Too much, or a very potent cinnamon.
items.fix: Half a teaspoon is correct. If the flavour is still too strong after cooking, add more lemon juice — acidity balances the cinnamon.

items.cause: Tinned chickpeas that were added too early and have dried out, or underdrained beans.
items.fix: Rinse tinned chickpeas thoroughly and add them at the same time as the lentils. They only need to heat through, not cook.

content.text: Harira is the soup that marks time in Morocco. During Ramadan it is served at iftar (the meal that breaks the fast at sunset) every evening for a month, eaten with dates and chebakia (honey pastries). Outside Ramadan it is served at weddings and family gatherings, and in restaurants and homes throughout the year. The name probably comes from the Arabic word for silk, a reference to the smooth texture the dissolved lentils and chickpeas give the finished soup. Each family has its own version; additions of flour for extra thickness, beaten egg for richness, and lamb pieces are all common.
